--- SOURCE 3: Green Heterogeneous Catalysis for CO 2 Reduction - MDPI ---
URL: https://www.mdpi.com/journal/catalysts/special_issues/3D35198972

SUMMARY:
Special Issues, Collections and Topics in MDPI journals This Special Issue, entitled “Green Heterogeneous Catalysis for CO2 Reduction”, will present original research articles, reviews, and perspectives, based on both experimental and/or computational studies*,* reflecting the state of the art and future applications in this field, with emphasis on *green* processes that reduce the environmental impact of chemical processes and products, fostering energy efficiency, the use of renewable energy and raw materials, less hazardous synthesis methods and low by-product formation. Accepted papers will be published continuously in the journal (as soon as accepted) and will be listed together on the special issue website. *Catalysts* is an international peer-reviewed open access monthly journal published by MDPI. **Further information on MDPI's Special Issue policies can be found** here.

FULL CONTENT:
All articles published by MDPI are made immediately available worldwide under an open access license. No special
permission is required to reuse all or part of the article published by MDPI, including figures and tables. For
articles published under an open access Creative Common CC BY license, any part of the article may be reused without
permission provided that the original article is clearly cited. For more information, please refer to
<https://www.mdpi.com/openaccess>.

Feature papers represent the most advanced research with significant potential for high impact in the field. A Feature
Paper should be a substantial original Article that involves several techniques or approaches, provides an outlook for
future research directions and describes possible research applications.

Feature papers are submitted upon individual invitation or recommendation by the scientific editors and must receive
positive feedback from the reviewers.

Editor’s Choice articles are based on recommendations by the scientific editors of MDPI journals from around the world.
Editors select a small number of articles recently published in the journal that they believe will be particularly
interesting to readers, or important in the respective research area. The aim is to provide a snapshot of some of the
most exciting work published in the various research areas of the journal.

## Special Issue Information

Dear Colleagues,

A global response has become essential to mitigate climate change by reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions, specifically carbon dioxide (CO2), the primary GHG emitted through human activities. Some initiatives to address this environmental issue promote solutions to achieve neutrality in GHG emissions by 2050.

The generation and use of electricity from renewable sources is one of the most important strategies for reducing CO2 emissions in this net zero emissions scenario. This more sustainable alternative is relevant in transport, energy and industry sectors. However, in the transition to a carbon-neutral economy, and even in the new scenario, it is necessary to reduce CO2 emissions from biomass conversion and cement and steel industries.

A key approach to achieve this goal is the development of innovative and efficient catalytic processes to produce fuels and/or chemicals by CO2 reduction, as an alternative to processes based on fossil resources. The catalytic conversion routes to convert CO2 into the final product could be enzymatic, electrocatalytic, photocatalytic (or photoelectrocatalytic), and thermocatalytic.

This Special Issue, entitled “Green Heterogeneous Catalysis for CO2 Reduction”, will present original research articles, reviews, and perspectives, based on both experimental and/or computational studies*,* reflecting the state of the art and future applications in this field, with emphasis on *green* processes that reduce the environmental impact of chemical processes and products, fostering energy efficiency, the use of renewable energy and raw materials, less hazardous synthesis methods and low by-product formation.

Dr. Consuelo Alvarez-Galvan  
Dr. Marisol Faraldos  
Dr. Belén Bachiller-Baeza  
*Guest Editors*

**Manuscript Submission Information**

Manuscripts should be submitted online at [www.mdpi.com](https://www.mdpi.com/) by [registering](https://www.mdpi.com/user/register/) and [logging in to this website](https://www.mdpi.com/user/login/). Once you are registered, [click here to go to the submission form](https://susy.mdpi.com/user/manuscripts/upload/?journal=catalysts). Manuscripts can be submitted until the deadline. All submissions that pass pre-check are peer-reviewed. Accepted papers will be published continuously in the journal (as soon as accepted) and will be listed together on the special issue website. Research articles, review articles as well as short communications are invited. For planned papers, a title and short abstract (about 100 words) can be sent to the Editorial Office for announcement on this website.

Submitted manuscripts should not have been published previously, nor be under consideration for publication elsewhere (except conference proceedings papers). All manuscripts are thoroughly refereed through a single-blind peer-review process. A guide for authors and other relevant information for submission of manuscripts is available on the [Instructions for Authors](https://www.mdpi.com/journal/catalysts/instructions) page. [*Catalysts*](https://www.mdpi.com/journal/catalysts/) is an international peer-reviewed open access monthly journal published by MDPI.

Please visit the [Instructions for Authors](https://www.mdpi.com/journal/catalysts/instructions) page before submitting a manuscript.
The [Article Processing Charge (APC)](https://www.mdpi.com/about/apc/) for publication in this [open access](https://www.mdpi.com/about/openaccess/) journal is 2200 CHF (Swiss Francs).
Submitted papers should be well formatted and use good English. Authors may use MDPI's
[English editing service](https://www.mdpi.com/authors/english) prior to publication or during author revisions.

## Benefits of Publishing in a Special Issue

* Ease of navigation: Grouping papers by topic helps scholars navigate broad scope journals more efficiently.
* Greater discoverability: Special Issues support the reach and impact of scientific research. Articles in Special Issues are more discoverable and cited more frequently.
* Expansion of research network: Special Issues facilitate connections among authors, fostering scientific collaborations.
* External promotion: Articles in Special Issues are often promoted through the journal's social media, increasing their visibility.
* Reprint: MDPI Books provides the opportunity to republish successful Special Issues in book format, both online and in print.

**Further information on MDPI's Special Issue policies can be found** [here](https://www.mdpi.com/special_issues_guidelines).

URL: https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/term/green-catalysis-applications/

SUMMARY:
Green Catalysis Applications, at its simplest, refers to the utilization of catalysts to make chemical processes more environmentally

FULL CONTENT:
![Energy → Sustainability Directory](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Energy-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)
![Energy → Sustainability Directory](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Energy-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)
![Green Catalysis Applications](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/green-catalysis-applications-term-image.webp)

# Green Catalysis Applications

![Intricate interconnected rings and a central sphere symbolize sustainable systems emphasizing resource efficiency and circularity within energy flow. Metallic blue hues represent renewable resources and clean energy transition. The beige element embodies eco-friendly sustainable materials in contrast to the golden details suggesting resource optimization and the value in waste reduction for a carbon neutral future.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/interwoven-rings-reflect-sustainable-systems-and-circular-energy-flow-for.jpg "Interwoven Rings Reflect Sustainable Systems and Circular Energy Flow for Resource Efficiency")

## Fundamentals

**Green Catalysis Applications**, at its simplest, refers to the utilization of **catalysts** to make chemical processes more environmentally friendly and sustainable, particularly within the realm of **energy production and utilization**. To grasp this concept, let’s first consider what a catalyst is. In essence, a catalyst is a substance that accelerates a chemical reaction without being consumed in the process. Think of it as a facilitator, or a matchmaker, in the world of molecules.

It brings reacting molecules together in a way that lowers the energy barrier required for them to transform into new products. This facilitation is fundamental to many industrial processes, but traditional catalysis often involves harsh conditions, toxic materials, and generates substantial waste.

Green Catalysis Applications uses catalysts to minimize environmental impact and maximize resource efficiency in energy-related processes.

The ‘green’ aspect then comes into play. **Green chemistry** principles aim to design chemical processes that reduce or eliminate hazardous substances. **Green catalysis** is a direct application of these principles within the field of catalysis. Its **definition** encompasses the design and implementation of catalytic systems that are environmentally benign, resource-efficient, and sustainable.

This means focusing on catalysts that are less toxic, derived from renewable resources if possible, operate under milder conditions (reducing energy consumption), and produce less waste or, ideally, valuable byproducts. **Explanations** of [green catalysis Meaning → Green catalysis represents the design of chemical processes that minimize environmental impact by employing catalysts that are environmentally benign.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/area/green-catalysis/)  often highlight its role in reducing reliance on fossil fuels and mitigating pollution associated with energy production and chemical manufacturing.

![Close up view of precision engineered industrial components, their dark metallic surfaces reflecting in a liquid below, emphasizes the intricate nature of modern sustainable manufacturing processes. This machinery likely represents a stage in automated industrial production, showcasing efficiency and energy conservation principles. The image highlights metal components part of a larger system focused on optimizing resource management and reducing energy consumption in manufacturing.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/precision-engineered-industrial-components-reflecting-sustainable-energy.jpg "Precision Engineered Industrial Components Reflecting Sustainable Energy Manufacturing Processes")

### The Significance for Energy Sustainability

The **meaning** of Green Catalysis Applications is profoundly linked to the global pursuit of **energy sustainability**. Current energy systems, heavily reliant on fossil fuels, are unsustainable due to resource depletion and environmental damage, most notably [climate Meaning → The Climate domain addresses the science, impacts, and policy solutions related to global climate change and adaptation.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/)  change. **Descriptions** of this unsustainability are readily available, outlining the detrimental effects of greenhouse gas emissions from burning fossil fuels.

Green catalysis provides pathways to develop cleaner and more [sustainable energy Meaning → Sustainable energy refers to forms of energy that are inexhaustible and cause minimal environmental harm, crucial for long-term ecological balance.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/area/sustainable-energy/)  alternatives. It impacts [energy sustainability Meaning → Utilizing energy responsibly for current needs without compromising future generations’ access to clean and affordable energy.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/term/energy-sustainability/)  in several key ways:

![A detailed isometric view presents a technologically advanced modular power plant, meticulously designed to maximize solar energy capture for sustainable applications. The futuristic architecture integrates solar panels as core elements, showcasing a commitment to renewable energy sources. Its layered construction, featuring beige and dark colored blocks interconnected with intricate details, implies a scalable and adaptable energy solution for urban environments.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/futuristic-modular-solar-power-plant-architecture-for-urban-sustainability-and.jpg "Futuristic Modular Solar Power Plant Architecture for Urban Sustainability and Energy Independence")

### Examples of Green Catalysis in Energy

To solidify the understanding, consider a few tangible examples that provide **specification** of Green Catalysis Applications in energy:

These examples offer a glimpse into the breadth of Green Catalysis Applications in energy. The underlying **intention** is consistently to improve energy processes, making them cleaner, more efficient, and aligned with the principles of sustainability. The development and deployment of green catalysts are not just incremental improvements but are transformative steps towards a more sustainable energy future.

Green catalysis seeks to transform energy production and usage, shifting from polluting methods to clean and efficient technologies through innovative catalytic solutions.

![This intimate image details a robust arrangement crafted from fractured, charred wood, enveloping two round, light-colored objects, likely root vegetables. The intense lighting emphasizes the coarse texture of the burnt wood and suggests a basic yet effective approach to cooking with fire. It's a visual representation of utilizing biomass energy sourced from wood transformed into charcoal, highlighting reliance on sustainable resources.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/charred-wood-structure-utilizing-thermal-energy-for-sustainable-cooking.jpg "Charred Wood Structure Utilizing Thermal Energy for Sustainable Cooking Resources")

## Intermediate

Building upon the foundational understanding, we now move to an intermediate examination of **Green Catalysis Applications** within the context of energy sustainability. At this level, we will scrutinize specific catalyst types, reaction mechanisms, and the intricate challenges and opportunities inherent in this domain. The **sense** of green catalysis, beyond mere environmental friendliness, lies in its potential to fundamentally reshape energy landscapes. Let’s explore the nuances.

![A detailed view of a sustainable design object composed of concentric circles in blue and gold. The object exemplifies energy efficiency through thoughtful material selection and optimized resource management. Its construction suggests durability and longevity minimizing waste and promoting a circular economy approach.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-design-object-showcasing-energy-efficient-material-and-resource.jpg "Sustainable Design Object Showcasing Energy Efficient Material and Resource Management Practices")

### Catalyst Modalities in Green Energy Applications

**Explanation** of catalyst types is essential for a deeper understanding. Green catalysis doesn’t favor one catalyst type exclusively, but rather prioritizes catalytic systems based on their overall environmental performance and efficacy for specific energy applications. **Interpretation** of common categories reveals:

![A compelling perspective highlights the convergence of heavy industry and renewable energy. The image emphasizes sustainable infrastructure at ground level against a backdrop of wind turbines generating clean energy. The juxtaposition powerfully illustrates the ongoing energy transition and the integration of green technology in energy production.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-energy-infrastructure-juxtaposed-with-renewable-wind-power.jpg "Sustainable Energy Infrastructure Juxtaposed with Renewable Wind Power Generation for Clean Energy Future")

### Challenges and Opportunities in Green Catalysis for Energy

While the promise of Green Catalysis Applications is substantial, several challenges and opportunities define the trajectory of research and development. **Description** of these facets is crucial for realistic appraisal:

![A futuristic module representing sustainable infrastructure is displayed, featuring a robust design built with eco-friendly materials for long-term use and minimal waste reduction. The cube structure suggests modularity and scalability for advanced energy storage or renewable energy power generation systems, key components for grid modernization. Transparent sections offer a visual into its complex inner workings, possibly related to energy conservation or smart grids integration.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/advanced-sustainable-energy-infrastructure-for-grid-modernization-featuring.jpg "Advanced Sustainable Energy Infrastructure for Grid Modernization Featuring Future Energy Storage Technology Utilizing Eco Friendly Materials")

#### Challenges

![This close-up perspective showcases a bronze toned metallic industrial component, potentially a key element in energy infrastructure emphasizing sustainable resource utilization. The pronounced wear on its deep blue coating, exhibiting significant flaking and damage, underscores the tangible nature of material lifespan in demanding applications. This degradation visually represents the delicate balance between durability requirements and the ongoing need to mitigate resource depletion in a circular economy.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/worn-bronze-industrial-component-exemplifies-material-lifespan-for-energy.jpg "Worn Bronze Industrial Component Exemplifies Material Lifespan for Energy Infrastructure and Sustainable Resources")

#### Opportunities

Green catalysis for energy is in a dynamic phase, pushing boundaries to overcome challenges while leveraging immense opportunities for a sustainable future.

![This abstract composition showcases beige spheres and dark geometric shapes symbolizing sustainable energy. The image evokes clean energy with its minimalist design and suggests energy abstraction for visual appeal. It represents renewable resources in a stylized form, indicating sustainable development and clean technology advancements.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-energy-structures-for-sustainable-development-emphasizing-renewable.jpg "Abstract Energy Structures for Sustainable Development Emphasizing Renewable Resources and Minimalist Design")

## Academic

Moving into the academic domain, the **definition** of **Green Catalysis Applications** in energy [sustainability Meaning → Sustainability is the capacity for human civilization to coexist with the Earth's biosphere in a state of equilibrium.](https://sustainability-directory.com/)  undergoes a transformation from a functional description to a complex, multi-layered construct. Here, it is not merely about environmentally conscious catalysis, but about a **paradigm shift** in chemical reactivity and process design, driven by the imperative of ecological integrity and long-term resource management. The **meaning** now incorporates not only [environmental impact Meaning → Environmental impact is the alteration to the environment caused by human activities or natural events.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/term/environmental-impact/)  reduction but also socio-economic considerations, ethical implications, and systemic transformations necessary for a truly sustainable energy future. **Analysis** at this level necessitates engagement with diverse perspectives, rigorous methodological frameworks, and critical assessment of existing paradigms.

![This detailed macro photograph highlights an intricate spiral, suggesting a component within a sustainable energy technology. The composition, featuring a tightly wound coil-like form in shades of brown set against a gradient background of brown and blue, emphasizes depth and precision. The image subtly speaks to themes of energy efficiency, innovative solutions, and the importance of sustainable materials in modern industrial processes striving towards renewable energy transition.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/intricate-spiral-form-in-deep-hues-symbolizing-efficient-energy-systems-and.jpg "Intricate Spiral Form in Deep Hues Symbolizing Efficient Energy Systems and Sustainable Technological Innovation")

### Redefining Green Catalysis Applications → An Academic Perspective

The academic **interpretation** of Green Catalysis Applications transcends a simple substitution of ‘toxic’ catalysts with ‘benign’ ones. It demands a holistic and systemic view. Let’s dissect the layers of this academic understanding:

![This artistic arrangement of cuboid containers holding varied shades of wet paint serves as a visual metaphor for the critical elements within sustainable energy. The dark hues might represent traditional fuel sources moving towards cleaner, lighter tones, reflecting the shift towards renewable energy and sustainable energy practices. The moisture can symbolize water as a crucial resource in green energy generation and environmental conservation.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/exploring-sustainable-energy-resources-and-resource-management-in-a.jpg "Exploring Sustainable Energy Resources and Resource Management in a Contemporary Abstract Composition")

#### Beyond Benign Substitution → Systemic Redesign

The initial, simplistic **clarification** of green catalysis might focus on replacing hazardous chemicals in catalytic processes. However, an academic perspective pushes beyond this superficial level. It recognizes that true sustainability requires a fundamental redesign of chemical processes and energy systems. This involves:

![A close up of a heavy duty industrial component, possibly used within renewable energy systems, highlights principles of sustainable design and material science. Its robust construction and apparent durability emphasize longevity and resource conservation efforts, key aspects of eco-design. The component's weathered appearance may indicate an extended product lifespan within a circular economy, minimizing waste reduction and resource depletion.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/detailed-industrial-component-illustrating-material-durability-for-sustainable.jpg "Detailed Industrial Component Illustrating Material Durability for Sustainable Energy Applications")

#### Multicultural and Cross-Sectoral Influences on Meaning

The **meaning** of ‘green’ and ‘sustainable’ catalysis is not universally fixed. **Analysis** from a multicultural perspective reveals that definitions and priorities may vary across different cultures and geographical regions. For **instance**, in rapidly industrializing nations, the immediate priority might be pollution reduction to address pressing public health concerns, while in developed nations, the focus might be more on long-term climate change [mitigation Meaning → Mitigation focuses on efforts to reduce or prevent the emission of greenhouse gases, tackling the root causes of climate change.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/)  and resource security. **Description** of these varying perspectives acknowledges that the ‘greenest’ catalytic solution in one context might not be the same in another.

Cross-sectoral influences further shape the **interpretation**. The pharmaceutical industry’s criteria for ‘green’ might differ from those of the bulk chemical industry or the energy sector, reflecting varying regulatory landscapes, economic drivers, and social expectations. The academic discourse must acknowledge and critically examine these diverse influences to arrive at a globally relevant and nuanced understanding of Green Catalysis Applications.

#### In-Depth Analysis → Green Catalysis for CO2 Capture and Conversion

To provide a concrete in-depth academic **elucidation**, let’s focus on Green Catalysis Applications for **CO2 capture and conversion**. Carbon dioxide, a primary greenhouse gas, is a significant byproduct of energy production and industrial processes. From an academic standpoint, CO2 is not merely a waste product but a potentially valuable C1 feedstock.

Green catalysis offers pathways to capture CO2 from point sources (like power plants) or even directly from the air (direct air capture) and then catalytically convert it into fuels, chemicals, or materials. This addresses both climate change mitigation and resource utilization.

##### Catalytic CO2 Conversion Pathways

Captured CO2 needs to be converted into useful products. Green catalysis is central to developing efficient and selective catalytic pathways for CO2 conversion. **Statement** of prominent conversion routes includes:

The academic examination of Green Catalysis Applications for CO2 capture and conversion highlights the complexity and depth of this field. It moves beyond simple ‘green’ labeling and delves into the fundamental scientific and engineering challenges, while considering the broader systemic and socio-economic implications. The **essence** of academic pursuit in this area is to not just improve existing processes incrementally, but to fundamentally reimagine chemical reactivity and process design, driven by the principles of sustainability and a holistic understanding of environmental and societal needs.

Academic research in green catalysis for energy aims to fundamentally transform chemical and energy systems, aligning them with principles of sustainability, circularity, and long-term ecological well-being, driven by rigorous scientific inquiry and interdisciplinary collaboration.

Utilization of bio-inspired catalyst for CO 2 reduction into green fuels: Recent advancement and future perspectives
====================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Pragya Singh, Rohit Srivastava

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Share

Cite

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcou.2021.101748](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcou.2021.101748 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2212982021003152&orderBeanReset=true)

Highlights
----------

*   •The review focus on searching for alternative, effective, fast and economical routes for the reduction of CO 2 into green fuels with an emphasis on the associated chemical pathways. 
*   •This review focus on the utilization of various types of bio-catalysts for the reduction of CO 2 into various hydrocarbon products. 
*   •Synthesis processes/mechanisms of naturally and biologically inspired synthetic enzyme- based catalyst with emphasis on the nano-engineering perspectives or the selection criteria of process parameters that can lead to enzymatic approach with well correlated structures, properties and chemical functionalities. 
*   •Existing challenges in this domain and the future perspectives. 

Abstract
--------

Exhaustion of hydrocarbon deposits and increasing the level of CO 2 in the atmosphere is a very perturbing problem. Due to the greenhouse effect and pollution, the level of CO 2 increases rapidly. CO 2 considered as a greenhouse gas as well as basic raw materials for carbon resources. Therefore, CO 2 reduction and its utilization is a current topic of research. One of the best utilization methods of CO 2 is to convert into various products and hydrocarbons _via_ several techniques such as thermal, photochemical, electrochemical and enzymatic. In this review, we summarize the abstraction and working of CO 2 reduction into hydrocarbons fuels using the enzymatic approach. Proteins and microorganism based enzymes have been studied widely for the CO 2 reduction _via_ the redox reaction. The enzymatic approach is a green, less toxic and efficient way to reduce CO 2 into useful products emphasizing on Co-factor, single enzyme and multi-enzyme system for CO 2 reduction to various products and hydrocarbon fuels. Mechanism of enzymatic system integrated with polymer, transition metal and semiconductor (i.e. Nano-hybrids) and also enzyme immobilization also plays an important role for CO 2 reduction application were reviewed and discussed in detail. In this review, we also discuss the various types of enzyme system integrated with electro/photo catalyst and the implementation of the enzyme based catalysts in the semi-artificial photosynthesis system and mimic natural photosystem II. Finally, the new challenges and the novel approaches of CO 2 reduction _via_ the enzymatic catalyst are discussed.

Introduction
------------

Climate variation is a problem and worldwide subject for our environment and seeking attention towards the strategy makers, environmental-related academic researchers and for other bodies. Climate change creates problems for our environment such as global warming, environmental fracas, and social, financial and technological complications. Due to the several issues, greenhouse gases emission considered as the wide-ranging subject for the world [1]. Currently, greenhouse gas reduction is a substantial topic for a worldwide academic researcher.

There are six main types of greenhouse gas which gave directly impact on the environment such as CO 2 (Carbon dioxide), CH 4 (Methane), N 2 O (Nitrous Oxide), HFCs (Hydrofluorocarbons), PFCs (Perfluorocarbons) and SF 6 (Sulphur hexafluoride). Among all of them, carbon dioxide plays a vital role in climate change and environmental pollution [2]. For example, from 1990 to 2014, CO 2 emission is increased by approximately 60–70 % [3].

The global greenhouse gas (GHG) emission in the year of 2010 reached around 50 billion tons of CO 2- equivalents. It is predicted that the global population will be around 9 billion and by 2020 the demand of energy will increase gradually [4]. Carbon dioxide is the largest component of anthropogenic GHG emission. The government policymaker introducing limits on CO 2 and this led to face a problem for the industrial sector to control the CO 2 emission [5]. Throughout the world, the stationary sources are emitting almost 60 % CO 2 in the environment of the total fossil fuel equivalent to 23 Gt CO 2 per year [6].

The Intergovernmental Panel on climate change (IPCC) gives a guideline to all developing countries and industrial countries to reduce the CO 2 emission in the environment [6]. As per IPCC guideline, the target reduction for developing countries is 30 % [7] and 80 % for industrial countries [5] by 2050 to avoid the adverse effect on climate. The scientists across the globe have agreed that CO 2 emission gave many dangerous effects on the environment and weather such as monsoon disturbance and climate change [8]. The European Union set the reduction target at 20 % by 2020 and will be increased by around 80 % by 2050. Similarly, the US also adopts the reduction target of 80 % by 2050 [9]. The CO 2 emission level in the atmosphere has increased from 280 to 417.07 ppm currently as observed by Mauna Loa Observatory (HI, USA), [10]. The leading carbon emitter’s countries of the world are China, United States, Russia, India, the European Union and Japan [11,12].

The atmospheric concentrations of CO 2 are measured at the Mauna Loa Observatory (HI, USA) are exemplified in Fig. 1(a) and (b).

The first research paper was reported on carbon emission in 1981 [13]. Afterwards, it became a very concern topic throughout the world due to continuous increase of global warming. Drastically change in environment or climate change is another reason for the consideration of worldwide researchers and lawmakers for this efflux in carbon emission exploration. Allen et al. [14] review a study related to anthropogenic emission as the main reason for carbon emission and increases the globe temperature by about two degrees Celsius. West et al. [15] studied the carbon emission emits from agricultural waste. They also evaluate the impact that gives by the United States agriculture sector on carbon emission. They also explore the methods which help to reduce global carbon emission. Zhang et al. [16] studied the carbon emission rate in China from 1960 to 2007. Similarly, Soytas et al. [17] reviewed the relationship of energy consumption, income and carbon emission of the United States. Both authors investigated the effect of global carbon emission on their economy and took suitable action to consider.

Ibrahim et al. [18] gave attention to carbon emission and its reduction from the supply chain system. Supply chain management is a part of operation management in which all the negative impact on the environment is included such as emission, pollution and hazardous effect on the society. They discussed 30 research articles related to carbon emission and its reduction. They also explain the difficulties and performance indicators for reducing the carbon emission from each paper. Quéré et al. [19] accumulate the government policies and activity data to reduce the carbon emission in the COVID pandemic situation in the year 2020. The carbon emission is reduced by about −4 % to −7 % during the current pandemic situation.

Carbon emission and the reduction of CO 2 emission is a leading problem to solve in the environment.There are lots of sources which is responsible for CO 2 emission, not only industries but biological metabolism is also responsible for the CO 2 emission. Fig. 2 shows the carbon flux that is responsible for carbon emissions [20].

As shown in the above graph, it is necessary to reduce the CO 2 quantity present in the troposphere. Therefore, the academician and scientist introduces the CO 2 recycling concept to reduce the global greenhouse gas. CO 2 recyling introduces the many technologies to reduce the CO 2 from enviroment such as Carbon capture and storage (CCS) and carbon capture and recycle (CCR). The CCS technology is not economically favourable. On the other hand, CCR technology is cost-effective because it avoids the transportation cost for CO 2 storage. After the step of capture, it directly reduced into other valuable products [21].

In details, there are two types of approach to reduce the CO 2 emission i) Carbon capture and sequestration/storage (CCS), in this process, CO 2 stockpiled in deep rock cracks under ocean and land which does not utilize for the public purpose [22], and ii) Carbon Capture and Utilization/recycle (CCU). In the CCU approach, CO 2 is a capture from the atmosphere and reduced in various products. It can also be a substitute for fossils fuels [23,24]. Fig. 3 shows the archetypal examples of CO 2 capture, utilization and reduction technologies in the presence of the catalyst and split into two compounds such as i) C1 compound such as Carbon monoxide, formate, formaldehyde and methanol [25,26], and ii) catalytic organic synthesis using CO 2 as a starting material for carbon [27,28]. In the process of CO 2 reduction into artificials fuels such as hydrocarbons needs a high energy to initiate the reactions [29].

Carbon dioxide is a highly stable and non-polar molecule containing two double bonds with 1.17A° bond length between carbon and oxygen. CO 2 consist carbon atom in +4 oxidation state [30]. There are two types of CO 2 reduction reaction such as i) Single-electron reduction reaction and ii) Proton-coupled multi-electron reduction reaction.

CO 2 is thermodynamically highly stable molecules but mass transport and reaction kinetics are slow [31]. Due to this reason, a single electron reduction reaction needs high negative energy to activate the reduction reaction i.e. E° = −1.90 V [32]. During reduction of CO 2 to the various product, there are several steps such as electron and proton generate and transfer throughout the reaction, which means need more negative potential as compared to the theoretical voltage that is shown in Table 1 to activate the reduction reaction [[33], [34], [35]]. These overpotential could be disadvantageous because it decreases the efficiency and selectivity [35].

These overpotentials are introducing due to the various reasons such as resistance that generate in the system, slow kinetics mechanism and the mass transport limitation of the reactant molecules [39]. The over potential can be classified on three circumstances 1) Ohmic overpotential (η ohmic); 2) Activation overpotential (η activation) and; 3) Concentration Overpotential (η concentration) [34,40] as depicted in Fig. 4.

The ohmic overpotential (η ohmic) introduced due to the collision of the electron which is moving throughout the electrolyte and the electrode [41]. This overpotential depends on the types of primitive one element such as electrolyte and electrode. The activation overpotential (η activation) defines as the vital energy which directly takes part to activate the reaction and overcome the energy barrier to stabilize the reaction occurs at the electrode at a proper amount. The η activation depends on the type and excitability of catalyst takes part in the adsorption and desorption of products. The Last one, the concentration overpotential is arising due to the mass transport limitations such as the intrinsic diffusion of CO 2 rate [34,37,41,42].

Several Techno-economic analyses (TEAs) have been using to determine the economic stability of CO 2- reduced products over ten years. In this section, we evaluate the product performance from the marketing cost point of view [43]. Jouny et al. [44] briefly explain the economic stability of CO 2 reduction product in the Global market via the End-of-life net present value (NPVs). NPVs calculated from CO 2 electrolyser system that has various CO 2 reduction productions _i.e._ approximately 100 tons/day. According to Jouny et al. [44], the carbon monoxide and Formic acid is the only financially stable product in the market with NPVs of $ 13.5 million and $ 39.4 million. The products like alcohol such as ethanol n-propanol do not have much economically stable market value but may soon have a better worth in the souk.

Verma et al. [45] purposed a gross margin model to estimate the economic feasibility of the Carbon Monoxide, formaldehyde, methane and ethanol, productions. They also reported that the carbon monoxide and formic acid having a large economic market in future if some problems overcome such as maximum overpotential.

The market value of the CO 2 reduced products is not a profitable market as represented in the Fig. 5. Thus, the operational voltage utilization has to be minimum to increase the economic feasibility of the production of formic acid and carbon monoxide with other products such as methane, ethanol, methanol and ethane. Additionally, the electrochemical system has an anode & cathode catalyst that has a better quality to enhance the performance and production of CO 2 reduction products. Despite this, capital investment and economic feasibility still need more attention and consideration for more practical analysis [38].

To overcome the overpotential is one of the tasks in CO 2 reduction application. Many types of research are focussed on exploring catalyst that reduces the overpotential and increases the product selectivity and conversion efficiency [[46], [47], [48]]. Currently, it has attracted attention for reducing CO 2 even while mimicking artificial photosynthesis [49,50], thermochemical [51,52], photochemical conversion [53,54], and electrochemical techniques [55,56]. All these approaches need electron injection from an energy source such as light energy to activate the reaction. There is another method to provide an electron injection from permanent energy sources such as indirect by driving electrochemical processes or direct by irradiation [57]. To overcome the above problem, bio-inspired materials and bio-based material based on photosynthesis model and other biological approaches gained high interest. These all bio-inspired materials and bio-based materials directly used as a catalyst in photochemical, photo-electrochemical and electrochemical methods [[58], [59], [60]].

Now we gave attention on the biocatalyst such as enzymes and microorganism directly in the above application that is beneficial for the utilization in CO 2 reduction. Biocatalyst is innate enzymes based catalyst and has noteworthy application in the pharmaceuticals and drug intermediates. The main advantage of the biocatalyst is that it provides high chemo-selectivity, enantioselectivity, and regioselectivity [61], high selectivity, and high yield of the product even in mild condition. It can also be used the commercial-scale application of CO 2 reduction [62]. Especially enzymes have a remarkable advantage for this resolution, as they artificial produced and it does not necessitate any special supplements and also do not need any culture to store them like another microorganism. Another disadvantage of microorganism is requiring specific eco-friendly circumstances such as supplements, temperature and pH. Bacteriological CO 2 conversion primarily grounded on the fermentation process that is unfavourable for biofuel production [29]. Therefore, the extraction of enzymes from bacteria would be another option. However, characteristics of enzymes depend on the source of the enzyme or microorganism from which it isolated. For a specific reaction, a specific enzyme is used and a bacterial source has to be chosen correctly [63]. On the other hand, biocatalysts have some disadvantages too such as biocatalyst shows lower activity in aqueous solution, to eliminate this drawbacks by the immobilization of enzymes [64]. There are lots of advantages of immobilization enzymes are stability of catalyst is increases, reusability, and inhibition from contamination so we can use in pharmaceuticals and food industry application [65]. All the above points are useful to help the process cost and quality of the product also increased [66].

Enzyme immobilization has been processing via various techniques. These techniques are adsorption on solid supports [67], a covalent attachment via functional moieties [68], and entrapment of cross-linked enzymes with porous material [69] and encapsulation of enzymes in various kinds of oxides [70]. These techniques enhance the pH and temperature range for enzyme activity, thereby increasing the rate of reaction and yield of products [71]. A covalent attachment technique is one of the promising methods of immobilization. The functional supports such as polymers, porous oxides etc. along with linkers help to improve the activity of enzymes [72]. Currently, research is mainly concentrated in the field of biocatalysts for CO 2 reduction applications (i) the intake of two or more enzymes for better selectivity of products, (ii) better catalytic process and ambient conditions for enzyme immobilization [73].

Dehydrogenases enzyme seeking great attention in the CO 2 reduction application and we can use directly in the reduction reaction [74]. Dehydrogenase enzymes are using for converting CO 2 into various products such as CO, methanol, ethanol and formic acid under ambient condition in aqueous media. In this review, we discussed the CO 2 reduction to different products using different enzymatic and pathways.

Artificial photosynthesis/bio-photoelectrocatalyst
--------------------------------------------------

In this review, we discussed the natural enzyme which takes part in the CO 2 reduction reaction via the electrocatalytic [104,105,248], enzymatic [121,155,259], Photocatalytic integrated system [152,196,228] and bio mimic photosystem [121,263,264] approaches. In all approaches, the semiconducting material work as a light-harvesting system or responsible for electron transfer in the system and natural enzyme work as the catalytic centre. Conclusion, future aspects and challenges
-----------------------------------------

Oxidoreductase enzymes reported a powerful tool to convert the CO 2 into valuables products such as CO, HCOOH, CH 4 etc. As discussed above, methanol is the most studies product form of CO 2 reduction. Using different approaches, the researchers developed a various system for enhancing the bio-catalytic reaction such as immobilization of enzymes with different type’s nanoparticles such as TiO 2, CdS, graphene etc., optimization of reaction mechanism and conditions. enriched_points.expanded_content: Green catalysis refers to the use of catalytic processes that minimize harmful environmental impacts while enhancing sustainability in chemical reactions and industrial applications.
Green catalysis plays a crucial role in CO₂ reduction by enabling efficient transformation of CO₂ into valuable products, thus helping mitigate climate change and reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
Sustainable methods in green catalysis include utilizing renewable resources, milder reaction conditions, and less toxic catalysts, which reduce waste and energy consumption in chemical processes.
